function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Distinguish precisely among Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer, which has the highest count, and why?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing, specifically about Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. I need to find the definitions and distinctions among these terms in the filing.

Let me start by parsing the S-1 filing and then retrieving information about these terms.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of a Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported account in {{spacex_s1}}? How does the filing distinguish among these terms?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing preamble, here are the definitions for a **Starlink Subscriber**, **Service Line**, and **supported account**, along with how the filing distinguishes among them:

**1. Service Line**
*   **Definition:** An individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan. It is generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal (or group of terminals) and is billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. 
*   **Key Distinction:** The filing explicitly notes that the number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons. Multiple people (such as a household) can share a single Service Line, and conversely, a single person or business can maintain multiple Service Lines (e.g., having both a Residential and a Roam plan counts as two Service Lines).

**2. Starlink Subscriber**
*   **Definition:** A unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that *does not* have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.
*   **Key Distinction:** A Starlink Subscriber is essentially a subset of a Service Line. While a Service Line represents the actual provisioning of the internet service, it only counts as a "Starlink Subscriber" in their metrics if it belongs to a standard consumer or small-to-medium business account. It specifically excludes managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts (such as those in aviation, maritime, or government entities). 

**3. Supported Accounts**
*   **Definition:** Used in the context of the company's X platform and Grok AI services, a supported account refers to a human, bot, or similar account that has logged into the X platform or Grok. 
*   **Key Distinction:** This term is entirely separate from the Starlink ecosystem. While Starlink Subscribers and Service Lines measure physical internet connectivity provisioned to terminals, "supported accounts" measures digital logins on a software platform. Furthermore, the filing notes that supported accounts may include fake, spam, or bot accounts as long as they are active.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me get more specific information about these terms, including the actual counts and the precise language used in the filing.

arguments.prompt: Find the exact quoted definitions and counts for Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts in {{spacex_s1}}. Include the precise language used in the filing and any numerical figures reported for each metric.

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 preamble text, here are the exact quoted definitions and reported counts for Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts:

### 1. Starlink Subscriber
**Exact Quoted Definition:**
*   *"We define a Starlink Subscriber as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team."* 
*(Note: This is also restated in the glossary as: "“Starlink Subscriber” refers to a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.")*

**Reported Counts & Figures:**
*   **Q1 2026:** "Starlink Subscribers totaled approximately 10.3 million... in the quarters ended March 31, 2026" (up 105% year-over-year).
*   **Q1 2025:** "5.0 million... in the quarters ended March 31, 2025" (up 91% year-over-year).
*   **Full Year 2025:** 8.9 million
*   **Full Year 2024:** 4.4 million
*   **Full Year 2023:** 2.3 million
*   **Geographic Reach:** "approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets."

### 2. Service Line
**Exact Quoted Definition:**
*   *"A Service Line is an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users or physical persons."*
*(Note: The glossary version uses nearly identical language but specifies "Starlink User Terminal" instead of "Starlink terminal".)*

**Reported Counts & Figures:**
*   While a standalone numerical count for "Service Lines" is not explicitly tallied in a table, the filing establishes that the count of Service Lines directly mirrors the Starlink Subscriber count: *"We calculate Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period."* Therefore, there were approximately 10.3 million unique Service Lines as of March 31, 2026.
*   The text also clarifies the ratio of Service Lines to Subscribers: *"multiple service lines (e.g., both a Residential Service Line and a separate Roam Service Line, which would be defined as two separate Service Lines and therefore two Starlink Subscribers)."*

### 3. Supported Accounts
**Exact Quoted Definition:**
*   The provided text does not contain a formal, sentence-length glossary definition for "supported accounts." It is only presented as a metric within the AI platform discussion.

**Reported Counts & Figures:**
*   **Count:** *"Our integrated AI platforms across Grok and X have over 1.3 billion supported accounts active in the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026"*
*   **Related User Metrics:** This figure includes *"approximately 550 million MAUs [Monthly Active Users] and generating approximately 350 million daily posts."* Additionally, *"Of our MAUs, we had approximately 117 million MAUs that used Grok’s AI features as of March 31, 2026."*

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a good understanding of the three terms. Let me also check if there's any additional context about supported accounts and how they relate to revenue generation.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what is the relationship between supported accounts and revenue generation? Are supported accounts paying customers? Also, what is the relationship between Starlink Subscribers and revenue? Are Starlink Subscribers paying customers?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is the relationship between these terms and revenue generation:

**Supported Accounts**
The provided text does not mention or define the term "supported accounts." However, it does discuss "Starlink.com accounts" and "account holders." According to the text, a Starlink Subscriber is a unique Service Line directly assigned to a Starlink.com account. An account holder can maintain multiple Service Lines (e.g., a Residential Service Line and a separate Roam Service Line), and revenue is generated from the subscription fees billed to those individual Service Lines. 

**Starlink Subscribers**
Yes, Starlink Subscribers are paying customers, and they have a direct relationship with revenue generation. 
* **Definition:** A Starlink Subscriber is defined as a unique Service Line that is provisioned under a subscription plan and billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. 
* **Relationship to Revenue:** The company generates Connectivity segment revenue primarily through subscription fees from these consumer subscribers. The text explicitly notes that Starlink Subscriber ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) is calculated as the "service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during the period." 
* **Revenue Model:** Starlink Subscribers generate revenue through monthly subscription fees based on geographic market and download speed (recognized ratably over the service period), plus typically a one-time sale of a Starlink Kit.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find any discussion of supported accounts in the context of X platform, Grok AI, advertising revenue, subscription revenue, or paying users. Are supported accounts the same as paying subscribers? What is the difference between supported accounts and monetized users?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing text, **supported accounts are not the same as paying subscribers.** There is a significant difference between the two metrics, and the company treats them as distinct layers of its user base. 

Here is the breakdown of the discussion regarding supported accounts, paying subscribers, and monetized users based on the text:

### 1. What are "Supported Accounts"?
According to the glossary in the filing, **“supported accounts”** is the broadest metric used to measure reach. It refers to *"a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok."* 

Crucially, the filing explicitly states: *"The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active."* 

As of the twelve months ended March 31, 2026, the company reported **over 1.3 billion supported accounts**. This metric is used to show the massive scale of the platforms, but because it includes bots and inactive/fake accounts, it is not a direct measure of human engagement or revenue generation.

### 2. What are "Paying Subscribers"?
Paying subscribers are users who actively pay for premium features through X (Premium and Premium+) or Grok (SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, SuperGrok Lite) subscriptions. 

As of March 31, 2026, the company had approximately **6.3 million active paid subscribers**. This is a tiny fraction of the 1.3 billion supported accounts. The filing notes that the company is focused on converting a greater portion of its user base into these paying subscribers to increase the "penetration rate."

### 3. What is the difference between supported accounts and "monetized users"?
While the text does not use the exact phrase "monetized users," it outlines two distinct ways the company monetizes its user base, neither of which relies solely on the raw count of "supported accounts":

*   **Advertising Monetization (Active Users):** The company monetizes user activity through advertising on X. However, advertisers are not sold access based on "supported accounts." Instead, monetization is driven by **Monthly Active Users (MAUs)**—users who have actually interacted with the platforms at least once in a 30-day period. The company reported approximately **550 million MAUs**. The filing notes that ad revenue depends on "monetizable usage" and user engagement, meaning fake or bot "supported accounts" do not inherently generate ad revenue unless they are actively engaging with content in a way that satisfies advertisers.
*   **Subscription Monetization (Paying Subscribers):** This is direct revenue from the **6.3 million** users paying for X or Grok subscriptions. 

**Summary:**
*   **Supported Accounts (1.3+ billion):** Anyone or anything (including bots/spam) that has logged in over the last 12 months. It measures total scale, not revenue.
*   **Monthly Active Users (550 million):** The subset of accounts actively engaging with the platform monthly. This is the primary pool for advertising monetization.
*   **Paying Subscribers (6.3 million):** The subset of users who pay for premium features. This is the direct subscription monetization pool. 

The company's strategy explicitly states that rather than focusing primarily on growing the sheer number of accounts (MAUs/supported accounts), it is focused on "revenue and operating margin" and converting existing users into paying subscribers and driving advertising revenue per user.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive understanding of all three terms. Let me also verify the precise language used in the filing for the definitions and counts. Let me also check the glossary section to make sure I have the exact definitions.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the glossary definitions for "Starlink Subscriber", "Service Line", and "supported accounts". Quote the exact language used in the glossary section.

results.content:
Based on the glossary section of the provided text, here are the exact definitions for those terms:

• **“Starlink Subscriber”** refers to a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.

• **“Service Line”** refers to an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons.

• **“supported accounts”** refers to, when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active.

Now I have all the information I need to answer the question. Let me also verify the counts once more to make sure I have the right numbers.

Key facts:
1. **Starlink Subscriber**: A unique Service Line directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does NOT have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team. ~10.3 million as of Q1 2026.

2. **Service Line**: An individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons. The count of Service Lines is broader than Starlink Subscribers because it includes enterprise/government customers with negotiated agreements.

3. **Supported accounts**: When used in the context of the X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active. Over 1.3 billion as of LTM ended March 31, 2026.

Now to answer the question:
- Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer?
  - Starlink Subscriber is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer because it represents a unique Service Line that is billed according to Starlink's service plans. Each Starlink Subscriber generates subscription revenue.
  - Service Line is also revenue-generating (it's billed), but it's a broader category that includes enterprise/government customers with negotiated agreements.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the exact language about how Starlink Subscribers are calculated relative to Service Lines. Does the count of Starlink Subscribers equal the count of Service Lines? Are enterprise and government customers with negotiated agreements included in Service Lines but excluded from Starlink Subscribers? Quote the exact language.

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 text, here are the answers to your questions along with the exact quoted language:

**Does the count of Starlink Subscribers equal the count of Service Lines?**
Yes, but only for a specific subset of Service Lines. The count of Starlink Subscribers equals the count of Service Lines that are directly assigned to a Starlink.com account *without* a direct, negotiated agreement with the sales team. 

The exact language states:
* *"We calculate Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period."*
* *"We define a Starlink Subscriber as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team."*

**Are enterprise and government customers with negotiated agreements included in Service Lines but excluded from Starlink Subscribers?**
Yes. While the text defines a "Service Line" broadly as an individual instance of internet service, it explicitly excludes managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts from the "Starlink Subscribers" metric. 

The exact language states:
* *"Starlink Subscribers includes both Personal (e.g., Residential and Roam) and Business (e.g., Local Priority and Global Priority) subscription plans, but does not include managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in domains including aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites and government entities."*
* *"We define a Starlink Subscriber as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team."*

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a clear understanding. Let me also check if there's a specific count for Service Lines (which would include enterprise/government customers) versus Starlink Subscribers.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, is there a specific count or number reported for total Service Lines (including enterprise and government customers)? Or is only the Starlink Subscriber count reported? Also, find any discussion of how Service Lines relate to revenue, ARPU, or total connectivity revenue.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the answers to your questions:

**1. Is there a specific count or number reported for total Service Lines (including enterprise and government customers)? Or is only the Starlink Subscriber count reported?**

Only the **Starlink Subscriber count** is reported as a specific metric; there is no reported count for *total* Service Lines that includes managed enterprise and government customers. 

The text explicitly states that while a "Starlink Subscriber" is defined as a unique Service Line, this metric **"does not include managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in domains including aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites and government entities."** 

The only specific counts provided in the text are for Starlink Subscribers, which totaled approximately **10.3 million** and **5.0 million** for the quarters ended March 31, 2026, and March 31, 2025, respectively.

**2. Discussion of how Service Lines relate to revenue, ARPU, or total connectivity revenue:**

*   **Relation to ARPU:** Service Lines form the direct mathematical basis for Starlink Subscriber ARPU. The text notes that the company calculates "Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period." ARPU is then calculated by taking the "service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during the period divided by (i) the average number of Starlink Subscribers during the period and by (ii) the number of months in the period."
*   **Relation to Revenue Scale vs. ARPU:** The text highlights a strategic relationship between Service Lines (Subscribers) and revenue. The company's strategy is focused on "driving sustainable revenue growth... rather than prioritizing increases in ARPU." As a result, they expect ARPU to continue to decline as they expand internationally, meaning revenue growth is being driven by rapidly scaling the volume of Service Lines rather than extracting more revenue per line. 
*   **Relation to Total Connectivity Revenue:** Service Lines (via Starlink Subscribers) are the primary driver of consumer revenue, which represented "over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue" in 2025. However, because total Connectivity revenue also includes enterprise, government, and Starlink Mobile revenues (which are not counted as Starlink Subscriber Service Lines), the Service Line count only reflects the consumer/personal subscription portion of the total Connectivity segment revenue, not the entirety of it. Furthermore, the text notes that revenue could be even higher than the Service Line count suggests because "multiple people may share a single Service Line, including within a household," meaning the actual number of paying end users is "meaningfully higher" than the Service Line count.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the exact count of supported accounts, monthly active users (MAUs), and paying subscribers for X and Grok as of March 31, 2026. Also find the count of Starlink Subscribers as of March 31, 2026. Quote the exact language.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the exact counts and language for the requested metrics as of March 31, 2026:

**Supported Accounts and Monthly Active Users (MAUs) for X and Grok:**
"Our integrated AI platforms across Grok and X have over 1.3 billion supported accounts active in the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026, including approximately 550 million MAUs... Of our MAUs, we had approximately 117 million MAUs that used Grok’s AI features as of March 31, 2026."

**Paying Subscribers for X and Grok:**
"As of March 31, 2026, we reached approximately 6.3 million active paid subscribers, which was comprised of approximately 4.4 million X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers and approximately 1.9 million SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy and SuperGrok Lite paid subscribers."

**Starlink Subscribers:**
"As of March 31, 2026, we had approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers..."

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need to answer the question comprehensively. Let me organize my answer:

**Definitions (from the glossary):**

1. **Starlink Subscriber**: "a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team." Includes Personal (Residential, Roam) and Business (Local Priority, Global Priority) plans, but excludes managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts (aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, government entities). ~10.3 million as of March 31, 2026.

2. **Service Line**: "an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons." This is the broadest Starlink metric - it includes all billed instances, including enterprise/government customers with negotiated agreements. No specific total count is reported (only the Starlink Subscriber subset is reported).

3. **Supported accounts**: "when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active." Over 1.3 billion as of LTM ended March 31, 2026.

**Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer?**

Service Line is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer because:
- It's the actual billed unit ("billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service")
- It includes ALL customer types (consumer, enterprise, government)
- Every Service Line generates subscription revenue

Starlink Subscriber is also revenue-generating, but it's a subset that excludes enterprise/government customers with negotiated agreements. So it's a narrower measure of revenue-generating customers.

Supported accounts is NOT analogous to a revenue-generating customer because:
- It includes bots, spam, and fake accounts
- Only 6.3 million of 1.3 billion are paying subscribers
- It measures total reach/scale, not revenue

**Which has the highest count?**

Supported accounts has the highest count at over 1.3 billion, compared to ~10.3 million Starlink Subscribers (and Service Lines, which would be slightly higher than Starlink Subscribers but still in the tens of millions range).

**Why?**

Supported accounts has the highest count because:
- It's the broadest metric, measuring total reach/scale
- It includes any account (human, bot, or similar) that has logged into X or Grok in the last 12 months
- It explicitly includes fake, spam, or bot accounts if they are active
- It spans two large platforms (X and Grok) with massive global user bases
- It's a software/login metric, not a physical connectivity or billing metric

In contrast:
- Service Lines and Starlink Subscribers are physical connectivity metrics tied to actual billed Starlink terminals
- Each Service Line requires a physical Starlink User Terminal and an active subscription
- The count is naturally much smaller because it requires hardware deployment and payment

Let me now write up the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Distinguishing Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and Supported Accounts

The SpaceX S-1 filing (filed May 20, 2026) defines these three terms in its glossary, and they measure fundamentally different things — two relate to Starlink's physical broadband connectivity business, while the third relates to the X/Grok software platforms.

### 1. Precise Definitions (from the S-1 glossary)

**Service Line** — "an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and **billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service**. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons."

- This is the broadest Starlink-side metric. It captures *every* billed instance of service — consumer, business, enterprise, and government.
- The filing explicitly notes that one person/entity can hold multiple Service Lines (e.g., a Residential line plus a separate Roam line = two Service Lines), and conversely multiple people in a household can share one Service Line.

**Starlink Subscriber** — "a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that **does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team**."

- This is a **subset** of Service Lines. It includes Personal plans (Residential, Roam) and Business plans (Local Priority, Global Priority), but **excludes managed enterprise and government customers** with negotiated contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
- The filing states: "We calculate Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period" — but only counting those Service Lines that meet the non-negotiated-account criterion.
- Reported count: **~10.3 million** as of March 31, 2026 (up from ~5.0 million a year earlier).

**Supported accounts** — "when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. **The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active.**"

- This is an entirely separate metric tied to the X/Grok software platforms (acquired via the xAI/X Holdings mergers), not to Starlink connectivity.
- Reported count: **over 1.3 billion** supported accounts active in the LTM ended March 31, 2026 (vs. ~550 million MAUs and only ~6.3 million active paid subscribers across X Premium/Premium+ and SuperGrok tiers).

### 2. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer?

**Service Line** is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer.

Reasoning:
- The glossary definition explicitly ties a Service Line to billing: it is "billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service." Every Service Line is, by definition, a billed revenue-generating unit.
- It is the **most inclusive** revenue-generating metric on the Starlink side, capturing consumer, business, enterprise, and government customers alike.
- Starlink Subscriber is *also* revenue-generating (the filing computes Starlink Subscriber ARPU as "service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during the period" divided by average subscribers), but it is a **narrower** subset that deliberately excludes negotiated enterprise/government contracts. So while each Starlink Subscriber is a paying customer, the metric understates the total revenue-generating customer base.
- Supported accounts is **not** analogous to a revenue-generating customer. The filing itself warns that the count "may include fake, spam or bot accounts," and only ~6.3 million of the 1.3+ billion supported accounts are active paid subscribers. It is a reach/scale metric, not a billing metric.

### 3. Which has the highest count, and why?

**Supported accounts** has by far the highest count — **over 1.3 billion**, versus ~10.3 million Starlink Subscribers (and a slightly larger but still tens-of-millions figure for total Service Lines, which is not separately reported).

Why supported accounts is the largest:
- **Broadest definitional scope.** It captures *any* account — human, bot, or similar — that simply logged into X or Grok during the trailing twelve months. The filing expressly permits fake, spam, and bot accounts to be counted as long as they are "active."
- **Software/login metric, not a hardware/billing metric.** Unlike Service Lines, supported accounts require no physical terminal deployment, no subscription payment, and no negotiated contract — only a login event.
- **Spans two large consumer platforms.** X and Grok collectively reach a global user base orders of magnitude larger than Starlink's deployed terminal base.
- **Decoupled from monetization.** The filing discloses that only ~550 million of the 1.3+ billion are MAUs, and only ~6.3 million are paying subscribers — confirming that the headline supported-accounts figure vastly overstates the revenue-generating base.

By contrast, Service Lines and Starlink Subscribers are constrained by physical Starlink User Terminal deployment and active subscription billing, which naturally caps the count in the tens of millions rather than billions.

### Summary Table

| Metric | Definition (essence) | Revenue-generating? | Count (as of 3/31/2026 or LTM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Line** | Billed instance of Starlink service on a terminal | Yes — explicitly "billed according to Starlink's service plans" | Not separately reported; superset of Starlink Subscribers |
| **Starlink Subscriber** | Service Line on a non-negotiated Starlink.com account (excludes enterprise/gov't) | Yes — subset of Service Lines; ARPU computed from this base | ~10.3 million |
| **Supported accounts** | Any login (human/bot/spam) on X or Grok in trailing 12 months | No — includes fake/spam/bot accounts; only ~6.3M of 1.3B+ are paid | Over 1.3 billion |

**Bottom line:** Service Line is the closest analog to a revenue-generating customer (it is the billed unit and the most inclusive), Starlink Subscriber is a revenue-generating subset that excludes negotiated enterprise/government contracts, and supported accounts is a non-monetized reach metric that nonetheless posts the highest count because it counts any active login — including bots and spam — across the much larger X/Grok user base.

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) Form S-1, filed May 20, 2026 — glossary definitions of 'Starlink Subscriber,' 'Service Line,' and 'supported accounts'; Starlink Subscriber counts; X/Grok supported accounts, MAUs, and paid subscriber counts"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Distinguishing Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and Supported Accounts

The SpaceX S-1 filing (filed May 20, 2026) defines these three terms in its glossary, and they measure fundamentally different things — two relate to Starlink's physical broadband connectivity business, while the third relates to the X/Grok software platforms.

### 1. Precise Definitions (from the S-1 glossary)

**Service Line** — "an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and **billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service**. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons."

- This is the broadest Starlink-side metric. It captures *every* billed instance of service — consumer, business, enterprise, and government.
- The filing explicitly notes that one person/entity can hold multiple Service Lines (e.g., a Residential line plus a separate Roam line = two Service Lines), and conversely multiple people in a household can share one Service Line.

**Starlink Subscriber** — "a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that **does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team**."

- This is a **subset** of Service Lines. It includes Personal plans (Residential, Roam) and Business plans (Local Priority, Global Priority), but **excludes managed enterprise and government customers** with negotiated contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
- The filing states: "We calculate Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period" — but only counting those Service Lines that meet the non-negotiated-account criterion.
- Reported count: **~10.3 million** as of March 31, 2026 (up from ~5.0 million a year earlier).

**Supported accounts** — "when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. **The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active.**"

- This is an entirely separate metric tied to the X/Grok software platforms (acquired via the xAI/X Holdings mergers), not to Starlink connectivity.
- Reported count: **over 1.3 billion** supported accounts active in the LTM ended March 31, 2026 (vs. ~550 million MAUs and only ~6.3 million active paid subscribers across X Premium/Premium+ and SuperGrok tiers).

### 2. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer?

**Service Line** is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer.

Reasoning:
- The glossary definition explicitly ties a Service Line to billing: it is "billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service." Every Service Line is, by definition, a billed revenue-generating unit.
- It is the **most inclusive** revenue-generating metric on the Starlink side, capturing consumer, business, enterprise, and government customers alike.
- Starlink Subscriber is *also* revenue-generating (the filing computes Starlink Subscriber ARPU as "service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during the period" divided by average subscribers), but it is a **narrower** subset that deliberately excludes negotiated enterprise/government contracts. So while each Starlink Subscriber is a paying customer, the metric understates the total revenue-generating customer base.
- Supported accounts is **not** analogous to a revenue-generating customer. The filing itself warns that the count "may include fake, spam or bot accounts," and only ~6.3 million of the 1.3+ billion supported accounts are active paid subscribers. It is a reach/scale metric, not a billing metric.

### 3. Which has the highest count, and why?

**Supported accounts** has by far the highest count — **over 1.3 billion**, versus ~10.3 million Starlink Subscribers (and a slightly larger but still tens-of-millions figure for total Service Lines, which is not separately reported).

Why supported accounts is the largest:
- **Broadest definitional scope.** It captures *any* account — human, bot, or similar — that simply logged into X or Grok during the trailing twelve months. The filing expressly permits fake, spam, and bot accounts to be counted as long as they are "active."
- **Software/login metric, not a hardware/billing metric.** Unlike Service Lines, supported accounts require no physical terminal deployment, no subscription payment, and no negotiated contract — only a login event.
- **Spans two large consumer platforms.** X and Grok collectively reach a global user base orders of magnitude larger than Starlink's deployed terminal base.
- **Decoupled from monetization.** The filing discloses that only ~550 million of the 1.3+ billion are MAUs, and only ~6.3 million are paying subscribers — confirming that the headline supported-accounts figure vastly overstates the revenue-generating base.

By contrast, Service Lines and Starlink Subscribers are constrained by physical Starlink User Terminal deployment and active subscription billing, which naturally caps the count in the tens of millions rather than billions.

### Summary Table

| Metric | Definition (essence) | Revenue-generating? | Count (as of 3/31/2026 or LTM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Line** | Billed instance of Starlink service on a terminal | Yes — explicitly "billed according to Starlink's service plans" | Not separately reported; superset of Starlink Subscribers |
| **Starlink Subscriber** | Service Line on a non-negotiated Starlink.com account (excludes enterprise/gov't) | Yes — subset of Service Lines; ARPU computed from this base | ~10.3 million |
| **Supported accounts** | Any login (human/bot/spam) on X or Grok in trailing 12 months | No — includes fake/spam/bot accounts; only ~6.3M of 1.3B+ are paid | Over 1.3 billion |

**Bottom line:** Service Line is the closest analog to a revenue-generating customer (it is the billed unit and the most inclusive), Starlink Subscriber is a revenue-generating subset that excludes negotiated enterprise/government contracts, and supported accounts is a non-monetized reach metric that nonetheless posts the highest count because it counts any active login — including bots and spam — across the much larger X/Grok user base.
